  <title>Plugin and Bridge Use Different Bot Tokens - Conflict is Between 7 Duplicate Bridge Processes</title>

  </facts>
  <narrative>Token comparison definitively proves the previous hypothesis was wrong: the Claude Code plugin and standalone bridge are configured with DIFFERENT bot tokens (7950298450 vs 8931790494), meaning they operate completely independently with zero interference. The plugin's bun.exe processes maintain active Telegram connections to their own bot without issue. The user's recurring 409 "Conflict: terminated by other getUpdates request" error is purely self-inflicted by the standalone bridge: 7 separate node.exe instances (PIDs 41196, 31000, 39264, 39096, 42248, 25188, 30760) are all simultaneously long-polling bot 8931790494. Each start.bat launch spawned a new process that never terminated, accumulating duplicates over multiple restarts. Telegram's API allows only ONE getUpdates consumer per bot; with 7 competing for the same token, they continuously kick each other off. This grep is looking for spawn() calls in the bridge source code. The results show various spawn calls in different modules:
1. acpClient.ts - spawning commands for ACP client
2. backup.ts - spawning commands for backup operations
3. commands/status.ts - spawning electron for status app
4. daily-intel/podcast.ts - spawning processes for podcast generation
5. local-llm.ts - spawning llama.cpp server with cmd.exe /c start /min
6. pet-server.ts - reference to electron

The key finding here is the local-llm.ts spawn pattern: it uses `cmd.exe /c start /min` with `detached: true`, which creates a detached process that survives parent termination. This could be one mechanism creating orphan processes.

However, the main issue is still the 7 duplicate node.exe processes running src/index.ts. This grep doesn't show anything definitively about why start.bat creates multiple persistent instances, but it does show the codebase spawns various child processes, some detached.

This is investigation work to understand the codebase structure - useful context but not a major finding on its own.

<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Bridge Spawns Multiple Detached Child Processes That Can Survive Parent Death</title>
  <subtitle>Code spawns electron status UI and llama.cpp server with detached:true creating orphan-prone process trees</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>local-llm.ts spawns llama.cpp server via cmd.exe /c start /min with detached:true and stdio:ignore (line 57)</fact>
    <fact>commands/status.ts spawns electron UI with detached:true and stdio:ignore (line 45)</fact>
    <fact>Detached child processes survive parent termination becoming orphans</fact>
    <fact>Bridge creates multi-level process trees: start.bat → node.exe → potential detached children</fact>
    <fact>Closing terminal windows or killing parent processes leaves detached children running</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Source code analysis reveals the bridge architecture spawns detached child processes that persist independently of their parent. The local-llm.ts module launches a llama.cpp server using Windows-specific cmd.exe with /c start /min flags, explicitly setting detached:true and stdio:ignore. Similarly, the status command spawns an Electron UI window with identical detachment configuration. These patterns create orphan-prone process trees: when users close the terminal or Ctrl+C the main bridge process, these detached children continue running. While the immediate 409 conflict stems from 7 duplicate node.exe instances running src/index.ts, this detachment pattern explains why processes accumulate over multiple start/stop cycles - each launch potentially leaves behind orphaned descendants that the next launch cannot detect or clean up. The bridge lacks PID tracking or cleanup mechanisms like the Claude Code plugin's bot.pid approach.</narrative>
